text: Hey Surgical YouTubers, it's Kelly Slater here, the surgeon not the surfer. I'm a general surgeon in Brisbane, Australia. Today we're going to be talking about how I do a simple laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Please remember this video is for educational purposes only and if you're a patient, you must talk to your surgeon about your diagnosis and treatment. If you're a doctor, you must hold suitable qualifications to carry out this procedure.

The laparoscopic view reveals the gallbladder as a retractor is placed on its fundus and pushed towards the diaphragm. The cystic node and cystic artery are visible within Calot's triangle, along with the cystic duct and common bile duct. Important landmarks include Rivia sulcus and the base of segment four, with the common bile duct clearly visible at the base of segment four.

The surgeon identifies the anterior right hepatic artery running in front of the common bile duct, with the cystic artery branching directly from it. Minimal diathermy is used to incise the peritoneum on both sides of the gallbladder. The surgeon maintains tension on the gallbladder using a retractor on Hartman's pouch, ensuring effective cauterization. The Maryland Grasper is employed for quick and atraumatic dissection.

The surgeon uses the Maryland Grasper to carefully dissect behind the cystic artery, ensuring it is not confused with the larger right hepatic artery. The grasper is positioned parallel to the cystic artery and duct, and the cystic artery is divided early to open up Calot's triangle. Clips are placed low and high on the cystic artery to maximize the length for division.

The surgeon divides the cystic duct near the top clip, ensuring stability, and uses diathermy to clean the section between the cystic duct and liver, releasing strands to unravel the spiral valves. The gallbladder is dissected off the liver to confirm the critical view of the cystic duct. The posterior cystic artery is clipped due to its size. The cystic duct is dissected further using blunt dissection with a Maryland retractor, creating minimal bleeding. The distal end of the cystic duct is clipped for a cholangiogram, and the proximal duct is massaged to check for stones before introducing an Olsen-Reddick cannula.

The surgeon attempts to insert a catheter into the cystic duct, encountering resistance from the spiral valves. Using a Maryland dissector, the surgeon tries to dilate the duct to facilitate catheter entry. Despite initial difficulty, the surgeon makes a lower incision on the cystic duct to bypass the valve obstruction, successfully inserting the catheter and obtaining bile flow.

The surgeon removes the Olsen-Reddick cannula after achieving the cholangiogram and places two clips across the cystic duct, ensuring proper positioning. The cystic duct is cut, and additional tissue is divided using diathermy, with the hook directed upwards to avoid thermal injury. The gallbladder is detached from the liver by creating tension and using the diaphragm to facilitate removal. Any bleeding from cystic veins is cauterized, and the gallbladder is retrieved in a bag to prevent stone spillage. The abdominal cavity is irrigated, particularly in Rutherford Morrison's pouch, to remove bile and stones, ensuring the gallbladder bed is dry.
The surgeon withdraws the laparoscopic ports and inspects the surgical site for any bleeding before closing the incisions.
